title: Wally (Recommended)​
content: You will find the latest version of LemonSignal at https://wally.run/package/data-oriented-house/lemonsignal

title: Rbxm​
content: If you prefer the rbxm you can find it at https://github.com/Data-Oriented-House/LemonSignal/releases

title: What's LemonSignal​
content: LemonSignal is a pure Luau signal implementation that works both outside and inside Roblox without sacrificing performance, for Roblox it is aimed at replacing GoodSignal and BindableEvent, it has performance improvements over them and houses new features like variadics and the ability to reconnect disconnected connections using :Reconnect. More on those features on the next page.

title: API Design​
content: LemonSignal uses the conventional Roblox signal and connection so you can easily swap it with any other Luau signal that uses that API like GoodSignal and immediately enjoy the new features and improvements.

title: Reconnect​
content: There are occasions where you :Disconnect and end up calling :Connect again with a new function, with :Reconnect that stops being a problem because reconnecting uses that exact function you passed in your connection and simply links it back to the signal. info :Once connections retain their behavior, as in they will get disconnected again on the next :Fire local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection1 = signal:Connect(function() print("Used Connect") end) local connection2 = signal:Once(function() print("Used Once") end) signal:Fire() --> Used Once --> Used Connect connection1:Disconnect() signal:Fire() -- both connections are disconnected, nothing will fire connection1:Reconnect() connection2:Reconnect() signal:Fire() --> Used Once --> Used Connect print(connection2.Connected) -- prints false, the :Once connection retains its behavior signal:Fire() --> Used Connect

title: Variadics​
content: Another cool feature is the ability to connect a function while passing varargs which, for example, allows you to use the same function for all your objects for a clean and flyweight code. info :Fire varargs get appended to a connection's varargs, meaning that :Connect and :Once varargs will always come before :Fire's local signal = LemonSignal.new() local function foo(str1: string, str2: string) print(str1 .. " " .. str2) end signal:Connect(foo, "Hello") signal:Fire("world!") --> Hello world!

title: Connection<U...>​
content: </> interface Connection<U...> { Connected: boolean Disconnect: (Connection<U...>) → () Reconnect: (Connection<U...>) → () }

title: Connected​
content: This item is read only and cannot be modified. Read Only </> Connection.Connected: boolean Indicates whether the connection is connected or not. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Hello world!") print(connection.Connected) --> true connection:Disconnect() print(connection.Connected) --> false

title: Disconnect​
content: Method </> Connection:Disconnect() → () Disconnects the connection from the signal. May be reconnected later using :Reconnect(). local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Test:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") --> Test: Hello world! connection:Disconnect() signal:Fire("Goodbye world!")

title: Reconnect​
content: Method </> Connection:Reconnect() → () Reconnects the connection to the signal again. If it's a :Once connection it will be disconnected after the next signal fire. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Test:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") --> Test: Hello world! connection:Disconnect() signal:Fire("Goodbye world!") connection:Reconnect() signal:Fire("Hello again!") --> Test: Hello again!

title: Benchmark​
content: To verify which implementation is best, I made a barebones version of singly linked list, doubly linked list, unordered array and dictionary signals and benched them using boatbomber's bechmarker plugin. note The execution times are not meant to measure the absolute time it takes for a method to run, they are only meant to be used relative to eachother to see which one runs faster.

title: Conclusion​
content: We can conclude that a doubly linked list strikes the best balance out of the other 3 by having: Ordered fireFast iteration making :Fire run as fast as an arraySolves singly's O(n) disconnect by making it O(1) which makes it as fast as the other 2 All the signal implementations as ModuleScripts are here signals.rbxm All the .bench ModuleScripts that the benchmarker plugin uses are here benches.rbxm

title: Signal​
content: </> interface Signal { RBXScriptConnection:: RBXScriptConnection?, Connect:: <U...>( self: Signal<T...>, fn: (...any) → (), U... ) → Connection<U...>, Once:: <U...>( self: Signal<T...>, fn: (...any) → (), U... ) → Connection<U...>, Wait:: (self: Signal<T...>) → T..., Fire:: ( self: Signal<T...>, T... ) → (), DisconnectAll:: (self: Signal<T...>) → (), Destroy:: (self: Signal<T...>) → () }

title: RBXScriptConnection​
content: This item is read only and cannot be modified. Read Only </> Signal.RBXScriptConnection: RBXScriptConnection?  The RBXScriptConnection that gets made when using .wrap. It gets disconnected and removed when using :Destroy. local bindable = Instance.new("BindableEvent") local signal = LemonSignal.wrap(bindable.Event) local scriptConnection = signal.RBXScriptConnection print(signal.RBXScriptConnection) --> Connection signal:Destroy() print(scriptConnection.Connected) --> false print(signal.RBXScriptConnection) --> nil

title: new​
content: Constructor </> Signal.new() → Signal Returns a new signal instance which can be used to connect functions. local LemonSignal = require(path.to.LemonSignal) local signal = LemonSignal.new()

title: wrap​
content: Constructor </> Signal.wrap(signal: RBXScriptSignal ) → Signal Returns a new signal instance which fires along with the passed RBXScriptSignal. Players.PlayerAdded:Connect(function(player) print(player, "joined, from RBXScriptSignal") end) local playerAdded = LemonSignal.wrap(Players.PlayerAdded) local connection = playerAdded:Connect(function(player) print(player, "joined, from LemonSignal") end) playerAdded:Wait() -- Player1 joins after some time passes --> Player1 joined, from RBXScriptSignal --> Player1 joined, from LemonSignal connection:Disconnect() playerAdded:Wait() -- Player2 joins after some time passes --> Player2 joined, from RBXScriptSignal connection:Reconnect() -- Now we get our hands on more API! -- Player3 joins after some time passes --> Player3 joined, from RBXScriptSignal --> Player3 joined, from LemonSignal -- It can also be used to mock Roblox event fires. playerAdded:Fire(playerAdded, Players:FindFirstChildOfClass("Player")) --> Player1 joined, from LemonSignal

title: Connect​
content: Method </> Signal:Connect() → () Connects a function to the signal and returns the connection. Passed variadic arguments will always be prepended to fired arguments. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection1 = signal:Connect(function(str: string) print(str) end) local connection2 = signal:Connect(print, "Hello") signal:Fire("world!") --> Hello world! --> world!

title: Once​
content: Method </> Signal:Once() → () Connects a function to a signal and disconnects if after the first fire. Can be reconnected later. local signal = Signal.new() local connection = signal:Once(print, "Test:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") --> Test: Hello world! print(connection.Connected) --> false

title: Fire​
content: Method </> Signal:Fire() → () Fires the signal and calls all connected functions with the connection's bound arguments and the fired arguments. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Test:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") --> Test: Hello world! Behavior outside of Roblox When ran outside of Roblox, :Fire will halt its execution when a connection errors. local signal = LemonSignal.new() signal:Connect(function() error("error") end) signal:Fire() print("eof") -- this line wont be reached

title: Wait​
content: This is a yielding function. When called, it will pause the Lua thread that called the function until a result is ready to be returned, without interrupting other scripts. Yields Method </> Signal:Wait() → () Yields the coroutine until the signal is fired and returns the fired arguments. local signal = LemonSignal.new() task.delay(1, function() signal:Fire("Hello", "world!") end) local str1, str2 = signal:Wait() print(str1) --> Hello print(str2) --> world!

title: DisconnectAll​
content: Method </> Signal:DisconnectAll() → () Disconnects all connections currently connected to the signal. They may be reconnected later. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection1 = signal:Connect(print, "First:") local connection2 = signal:Connect(print, "Second:") signal:Fire("Hello world!") --> Second: Hello world! --> First: Hello world! signal:DisconnectAll() signal:Fire("Goodbye World!")

title: Destroy​
content: Method </> Signal:Destroy() → () Similar to DisconnectAll but also disconnects the RBXScriptConnection made using .warp. Contrary to the method's name, the signal remains usable. local signal = LemonSignal.new() local connection = signal:Connect(print, "Hello") signal:Fire("world!") --> Hello world! signal:Destroy() signal:Fire("Goodbye World!")
